title: GDPR Data Minimisation Compliance in Logs and APIs

compliance.assurance_rationale: Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).
Auto-migrated from cat-22 markdown on Phase 1.3 run. Requires SME review to confirm clause accuracy, mode (satisfies vs detects-violation-of), and assurance level (contributing/partial/full).

app: Splunk Edge Processor (Splunk Cloud Platform), HTTP Event Collector (HEC)
spl:
index=app sourcetype="api:json" earliest=-24h
| spath path=body{}
| fields _raw endpoint purpose
| rex field=_raw max_match=0 "(?<field>\"(ssn|dob|health|biometric)[^\"]*\")"
| lookup gdpr_field_classification.csv field_name OUTPUT sensitivity, allowed_purposes
| where sensitivity="special_category" AND NOT match(allowed_purposes, purpose)
| stats count by endpoint, purpose, field_name
| sort - count
value: Data minimisation requires limiting collection to what is adequate, relevant, and limited. This use case detects API and application log fields classified as excessive for the documented purpose — for example special-category fields in general application logs — using schema metadata and sampling.
implementation: (1) Build `gdpr_field_classification.csv` from data dictionary; (2) tune `spath`/`rex` to your JSON shapes; (3) alert API owners on minimisation violations; (4) route critical patterns to Edge Processor drop rules; (5) feed results into DPIA updates.
visualization: Table (violating endpoints), Bar chart (field frequency), Single value (daily violation events), Pie chart (by sensitivity class).
knownFalsePositives: Administrative tasks, scheduled jobs or platform updates can match this pattern — correlate with change management, maintenance windows and user role before raising severity.
